channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: It's Open Season for Dinosaurs in Turok: Origins
item.content:encoded:
Turok is a storied franchise. A bonafide 90s classic. The 2008 game? Well, we don't talk about that one. Since then, the series has been stagnant, with exception to Nightdive's Remasters. But with Turok: Origins, dino-shootin' action makes a return.

We got hands-on time with Turok: Origins at Summer Game Fest Play Days. While it can be played solo, up to two other players can join you in slaying prehistoric beasts and other monsters besides.

This session with Turok: Origins was with two others, which definitely seems like the way to play. It's a mission-based game that simply asks players to accomplish certain simple objectives, while tackling a boss at the end.

The first thing I noticed is the aesthetic of Turok: Origins. Lush jungles filled with alien flora and angry dinosaurs is to be expected, but it's a surprisingly polished game. Graphically, it's very polished, but even more impressive is how well it runs.

The developers made a point to talk about its performance being great, and not once did the FPS dip. That said, the specs on the PCs were likely pretty beefy, but for now, I have no reason to doubt that optimization is a priority for Turok: Origins' developers.

Our mission begins with the selection of three different classes: The Couger, Bison, and Raven. Unfortunately, I didn't get enough time to intuit what kind of niche they fit, but if I had to guess, Couger is an all-rounder time, while the Bison is more tanky, and lastly, the Raven focuses on precision.

This is conveyed both through the weapons each class uses, as well as their unique abilities. The Raven, for instance, has a precise bow that is powerful but requires proper aim to fully utilize, as well as a rocket launcher type gun and an accurate, beam-based weapon.

It was with the Raven that I had the most fun, as there's something exciting and authentically Turok using a bow and arrow to hunt dinosaurs; though, the Couger, which I also tried, has a crossbow.

And with each class are those abilities I mentioned, though again, due to the fast nature of the gameplay, I wasn't able to fully gleam info on these abilities. For the Raven, at least, I can say that three abilities on cooldown can make a difference while fighting a horde of monsters.

For instance, the Raven uses a bubble that slows enemies in its proximity, which is especially useful on a giant, deformed T-Rex boss with missile launchers attached to its back. Yep, we can't ignore the absurdity of the premise, and I love it for that.

Mission objectives seem very simple. For instance, one task barred the way of my comrades and I, requiring we destroy a few objects to disable a generator. One other objective has us defend an NPC from an onslaught of humanoids with guns, as well as pterodactyls.

Missions are very brief, with a window of around 15 minutes seeming to be the goal. I don't mind quick-fire missions, but I definitely hope to see more substance when it releases.

The last boss of our demo is a gargantuan brute. Maybe not quite a dinosaur, but a humanoid that towered above us and welded a fiery hammer. Its swings would cause rings of flame, necessitating we jump to avoid damage.

Also, here's a neat trick. Turok: Origins is both a first-person shooter and third-person, with the ability to seamlessly switch on the fly. I found myself enjoying both views, and for instances like those flame rings, choosing your preferred perspective can help. 

With the ability to scan, this supposedly shows enemy weak spots, but it didn't seem telegraphed enough for me to really gauge if I was hitting the right spots. And while we did defeat this foe, feedback from my guns weren't as strong as I'd have liked, though on normal enemies they definitely do the job.

Regardless, Turok: Origins looks like a fun visit back to an old franchise. It's not going to reinvent the wheel; rather, it's a game that seems like a fun time with friends, and maybe that's all it needs to do. And hey, if it reignites an interest in Turok, I'm all for it!

Turok: Origins was played during Summer Game Fest Play Days 2026.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/its-open-season-for-dinosaurs-in-turok-origins
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Mr. Records Mixes Rhythm Game, Shop Management, and Music Appreciation
item.content:encoded:
During the Day of the Devs stream after the big Summer Game Fest showcase, a cute little game called Mr. Records was revealed. It blends rhythm game and shop management mechanics to create a charming little title full of music appreciation.

George, the player character, is an older gentleman who's fulfilling his dream of opening a record shop. So by day, you run the shop, talking to customers and helping them find the perfect record based on clues they give.

At nighttime though, you relax in his room upstairs, managing different parts of the store behind the scenes. You take phone calls for story moments, buy more vinyls to sell, and listen to tracks to learn more about them. However, when George hears a song, his imagination comes to life.

Don't Skip on Mr. Records

When you play a song, you go into George's imagination, where he'll run from left to right through a scene that fits the song. The first song feels very Beatles inspired, so he runs through a green field with piranha plants (it's still a video game, after all). The funky track was more abstract, featuring bright colors and pinwheel backgrounds.

He runs automatically to the beat, and it's up to you to jump over pits, kick obstacles, and slide up or down to the prompts. It's essentially another way to read a song chart and understand when to do what input, and I found it pretty easy to wrap my head around. The level of difficulty isn't that high here, making it pretty approachable.

It never got so challenging for me that I failed a song, but there is a failure state in Mr. Records. He has a certain number of lives, and every miss ticks that down by one. Misses also lower your rank on the song, with a no-miss score yielding a perfect four stars.

Finishing a song on the normal difficulty unlocks hard mode, which I honestly found more engaging and fun. There's more prompts to different melodies and rhythms, so I was able to enjoy and pick up on the music a little more than the first time I played it.

In general, Mr. Records provides good opportunities to help you listen to music a little deeper than you might have before. Each song obviously pays homage to different genres and eras of music, and while I wouldn't replace real-life '90s rock music with one of the songs in Mr. Records, it's cool that the game gives you reasons to replay the song and understand its vibe. You can then extrapolate that to real songs.

I'm not sure how deeply the game will test players' knowledge, but the NPC interactions during the shopping segment has me curious about it. One customer asked for an album with a colorful cover featuring four characters. After delivering that vinyl, George recounted a little fun fact about how that song was recorded. It's not real, obviously, but it sounded like the sort of story that has definitely happened in real life.

Mr. Records has no confirmed release date at the moment, but you can wishlist the game on Steam. It's developed by Glee-Cheese Studio and will be published by Wired Productions.

Mr. Records was previewed on a PC at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Robert Scarpinito is the Features Editor of TechRaptor. With a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism from the Ohio State University, sharing compelling stories is… More about Robert



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/mr-records-mixes-rhythm-game-shop-management-and-music-appreciation
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
